title: Why I Wouldn't Take Glycine Before Bed

chunks.title: Glycine and Sleep
chunks.summary: The discussion focuses on glycine as a supplement for enhancing sleep quality. The speakers highlight that while glycine has positive evidence for sleep, caution is necessary for individuals with specific genetic polymorphisms affecting methylation.
chunks.transcript: Let's go um let's go diet for uh like for sleep here. Is there what are your what's your uh your take on glycine? There's a lot of interesting evidence on glycine for sleep. >> Yeah, I think the evidence is pretty solid. Yeah. >> You know, um I take magnesium 3 and8 before I go to sleep. That's the one that works best for me. But I think some form of magnesium before you sleep seems to have some pretty good data. And either glycinate or thrienate works great. >> What about even like the they've seen there some stuff on like just straight up three grams of pure glycine. >> Yeah, I've seen people do it. Yeah, it's sweet. I mean, glycine is an interesting amino acid. You know, the only thing I know with glycine that you just have to be aware of is that if you have polymorphisms for methylation, excess glyc glycine consumes methyl groups. So, you it interacts with methylation sometimes. So, that's the only thing to be aware of. If you don't have polymorphisms with methylation, you're not going to have an issue with it. But, I have to be a little careful

chunks.title: Dietary Impact on Sleep
chunks.summary: The speakers emphasize the role of diet in sleep quality, discussing how macronutrient intake during the day influences blood sugar stability at night. They share personal experiences about the timing of meals and the impact of protein intake on sleep.
chunks.transcript: MTHFR. I take a methylated B vitamin. And so I think that if I take too much glycine, I will it appears that I sort of like pull methyl groups from other things into the glycine because excess glycine is um it uses methyl groups when it's Yeah. >> What about uh you know obviously you're you're having quite a bit of carbohydrates, quite a bit of fruit, quite a bit of honey. Do you can you have that straight up to bedtime, no problem, or do you still limit before? >> I I I stop eating probably at least an hour or two before I go to sleep. Yeah. Although sometimes I mean I can eat a tablespoon of honey before I go to sleep and it doesn't hurt at all. I think there's some anecdotal evidence for people like taking honey right before they go to sleep. >> Yeah. >> And I So for me, like sleep is so interesting. Like what I do in the whole day affects my sleep, right? I'm really intentional about my food and you know, I try and get 160 grams of protein per day. But if I have a low protein day, uh I might not sleep as well because I might get a blood sugar dip in the middle of the night. So like getting enough calories, getting enough carbohydrates from good sources during the day, getting enough protein during the day, that all at least subjectively for me stabilizes my blood sugar at night and I sleep better when I get enough. Like so that's that's another thing to think about is like everything I'm doing during the day sets me up for

chunks.title: Environment and Sleep Quality
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the effects of environment on sleep, particularly the benefits of spending time outdoors. The speakers share anecdotal observations about how increased exposure to sunlight correlates with better sleep, regardless of stress levels.
chunks.transcript: just unfathomable diff difference between days when I spend a lot of time outside and just get a lot of sun and how I sleep. So, I've thought a lot about this cuz so many people have theories on the whole like, "Why do I sleep well on vacation?" Oh, it's because you're not stressed out. Okay? I mean, first of all, let me put this out on the table.
